channel.title: Gianni De Jaeger - E-Portfolio
channel.description: Recent content on Gianni De Jaeger - E-Portfolio

item.title: How This Website Was Created
item.description:
For my e-portfolio, I chose to build a static website using Hugo. Hugo is a static site generator where the content is written in Markdown files. This approach fits the assignment well, because every blog post can be maintained as a separate file.

The website is hosted using GitHub Pages. This means no separate paid hosting is required, while the website also remains accessible after graduation. The source code of the website is stored in a GitHub repository. Whenever I make changes, I can push a new commit, after which GitHub Actions automatically republishes the website.

I deliberately chose Hugo instead of a traditional CMS such as WordPress because Hugo is lightweight, fast and easy to manage. For this portfolio, I mainly need to publish written reflections and articles, making a full CMS solution unnecessary.

Adding a new post is straightforward. I create a new Markdown file inside the content/posts/ directory, add the title and date at the top of the file, and then write my reflection. After pushing the changes to GitHub, the website is automatically updated.

item.title: Tech \& Meet – The Defense Cybersecurity Landscape in Belgium
item.description:
Tech & Meet – The Defense Cybersecurity Landscape in Belgium

Later in December, I attended another Tech & Meet event focused on the cybersecurity landscape in Belgium. While the previous session explored cybersecurity from an international NATO perspective, this event focused much more on how Belgium itself organizes cyber defence and national cybersecurity efforts.

The session featured speakers from organisations such as the Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium (CCB), the Belgian Cyber Force / Cyber Command and EgovSelect.

Content of the session

The event aimed to provide an overview of the most important cybersecurity organisations operating in Belgium and explain how they contribute to protecting the country against cyber threats.

Several organisations and acronyms were discussed, including:

- CCB,

- CERT,

- Cyber Command,

- FCCU,

- NCCN,

- VSSE,

- and SGRS.

One of the goals of the session was to help participants “see the forest for the trees” and better understand the role of each organisation within Belgium’s cybersecurity ecosystem.

The speakers also explained:

- what skills are needed in cybersecurity roles,

- how recruitment works for defense and government positions,

- and what career paths exist within these organisations.

An important message throughout the event was that cybersecurity is not limited to “hackers” or purely technical experts. Belgium also needs:

- analysts,

- instructors,

- policy specialists,

- network experts,

- cloud specialists,

- researchers,

- digital forensics experts,

- and threat hunters.

Reflection

I found this event particularly valuable because it showed how broad the cybersecurity field actually is.

The session demonstrated that defending a country digitally requires collaboration between many different organisations and disciplines. Cybersecurity is not only about technology but also about communication, intelligence, operations, infrastructure and policy.

One interesting point discussed during the presentation was how modern military systems generate enormous amounts of data. For example, unmanned drones can produce multiple terabytes of data per minute. This illustrates how strongly cybersecurity and data processing are becoming integrated into defense operations.

Another point that stood out to me was that modern soldiers themselves are increasingly connected through digital systems, making them potential targets for cyberattacks as well.

I also appreciated the realistic explanation of defensive and offensive cyber capabilities. The speakers explained how cybersecurity within defense environments often requires understanding both sides in order to effectively protect systems and infrastructure.

Personal takeaway

This session gave me a much clearer understanding of the Belgian cybersecurity landscape and the many opportunities available within the field.

It was interesting to see how organisations such as the CCB and Cyber Command are actively expanding and investing in cybersecurity capabilities as cyber threats continue to increase, especially with the growing influence of AI-assisted attacks.

The event also reinforced the idea that cybersecurity will only become more important in the future, both nationally and internationally.

Would I recommend it?

Yes. I would definitely recommend this session to students interested in cybersecurity, networking, digital forensics or defense-related IT.

The presentations provided a very accessible overview of Belgium’s cybersecurity ecosystem while also showing the real-world importance and impact of cyber defence today.

item.title: Tech & Meet – The Birth of NATO’s Cyber Defence
item.description:
Tech & Meet – The Birth of NATO’s Cyber Defence

At the beginning of December, I attended a Tech & Meet evening session titled The Birth of NATO’s Cyber Defence, presented by ir. Martin De Pauw.

The session explored how cybersecurity evolved within NATO from a niche technical topic into a full operational domain alongside land, sea and air operations.

About the speaker

Martin De Pauw graduated from the Polytechnic division of the Royal Military Academy in 1989 and initially worked in F16 avionics and air traffic management software. Later in his career, he transitioned into software, hardware and networking environments and eventually became involved in helping shape cyberspace operations within NATO.

After his military career, he continued working at NATO Headquarters in IT and Cyber-related roles.

What made this presentation especially interesting was that the speaker was directly involved in the development of NATO’s cyber defence strategy and structures.

Content of the session

The presentation focused on how NATO gradually recognized cyberspace as a critical operational domain.

One of the key historical moments discussed was NATO officially recognizing cyberspace as a domain of operations in 2016. This decision reflected how cyber threats had evolved from isolated technical incidents into risks capable of impacting international security and critical infrastructure.

Several important cybersecurity topics and examples were discussed during the session, including:

  • the Stuxnet worm and its impact on industrial systems,
  • state-sponsored threat actors such as APT28 (“Fancy Bear”) and APT29 (“Cozy Bear”),
  • insider threats as one of the biggest cybersecurity risks,
  • business continuity during cyber incidents,
  • and the growing role of AI in offensive cyber operations.

I found the discussion around weaponized AI particularly interesting. The speaker explained how AI technologies are increasingly being abused by threat actors, including examples where AI agents were manipulated or jailbroken to assist cyberattacks.

Another important point was the protection of critical national infrastructure such as bridges, power plants and other essential systems. The presentation showed how cyberattacks are no longer limited to stealing data but can potentially impact physical infrastructure and daily life.

Reflection

I found this session extremely interesting because it connected cybersecurity with international politics, military strategy and global security.

Cybersecurity is often approached purely from a technical perspective in courses, but this presentation demonstrated how cyber defence also involves strategy, cooperation between nations and long-term resilience planning.

One message that strongly stood out to me was:

“Cyber has no borders.”

The session clearly showed how interconnected modern societies have become and how cyber threats can affect governments, businesses and civilians simultaneously.

I also appreciated the speaker’s realistic perspective on security. Technical protections are important, but the presentation repeatedly emphasized that people remain one of the weakest links in cybersecurity.

The event reinforced my interest in cybersecurity and threat intelligence, especially in the broader geopolitical and strategic aspects of the field.

Would I recommend it?

Absolutely. I would highly recommend this session to anyone interested in cybersecurity, networking, international security or cyber defence.

The combination of historical context, real-world examples and insider experience made this one of the most interesting Tech & Meet events I attended this year.

item.title: Tech \& Meet – The Transition to IPv6
item.description:
Tech & Meet – The Transition to IPv6

Last week, I attended a Tech & Meet evening session about IPv6 presented by Nico Declerck. The session focused on the ongoing transition from IPv4 to IPv6, the importance of dual-stack environments and the security considerations involved in modern network infrastructures.

The presentation was aimed at both networking professionals and students interested in understanding why IPv6 adoption is becoming increasingly important today.

About the speaker

Nico Declerck is an experienced network administrator and Linux system administrator. Besides working in networking and cybersecurity, he is also a lecturer in the Bachelor Applied Informatics and Bachelor CyberSecurity programmes at Howest University of Applied Sciences.

In addition, he is active as:

- researcher within Cluster IT,

- Cisco Networking Instructor,

- Cisco Instructor Trainer,

- and advisor for BiASC (Belgian IT Academy Support Center).

His practical experience in networking and cybersecurity clearly reflected throughout the presentation.

Content of the session

The event was divided into three major parts:

Why IPv6?

The session started with an explanation of the origins and limitations of IPv4. Topics such as address exhaustion and the rise of NAT were discussed to explain why IPv6 became necessary.

The speaker emphasized that the transition to IPv6 can no longer be postponed, especially as more ISPs, enterprises and now even SMEs are adopting IPv6 and dual-stack environments.

Transitioning to IPv6-only

The second part focused on future scenarios where IPv4 support may gradually disappear. Different transition strategies, risks and challenges were discussed, including compatibility and infrastructure concerns.

I found this section especially interesting because it highlighted that IPv6 is no longer simply “future technology”, but something organisations are actively preparing for today.

Dual Stack in Practice

The final part of the presentation focused on practical deployment strategies for dual-stack environments.

Topics included:

- routing,

- firewalls,

- IPv6 configurations,

- and security-by-design principles.

The practical examples helped make the technical concepts easier to understand and demonstrated how organisations can start implementing IPv6 incrementally instead of performing a full migration at once.

Reflection

I found this session very insightful because IPv6 is a topic that is often mentioned in networking courses, but not always explored from a practical deployment perspective.

One of the strongest messages from the presentation was that the transition is already happening. IPv6 adoption is no longer limited to large enterprises or ISPs; smaller organisations are increasingly being confronted with the need to support IPv6 as well.

The combination of technical explanations and practical examples made the session both educational and accessible. I also appreciated how security considerations were integrated throughout the presentation instead of being treated as a separate topic.

Another enjoyable aspect was the presentation style itself. Nico Declerck occasionally included anecdotes about Bruges, its history and local beers, which made the session more engaging and informal.

What I learned

This session helped me better understand:

- why IPv4 limitations led to IPv6 adoption,

- the role of NAT in modern networks,

- how dual-stack environments operate,

- and the security implications of IPv6 deployments.

It also reinforced the importance of staying up to date with evolving networking technologies, especially in cybersecurity and infrastructure-related fields.

item.title: Hack The Future – Deep Dive into Linux Waters
item.description:
Hack The Future – Deep Dive into Linux Waters

Recently, I participated in Hack The Future in Antwerp, a full-day hackathon organised by De Cronos Groep. The event brought together students and companies for a day focused on coding, collaboration, troubleshooting and problem solving through a variety of technical challenges.

Together with a teammate, I worked on Challenge 112: *Deep Dive into Linux Waters*, organised by Piros / QUBR.

The challenge

The challenge focused on Linux, automation and containerization. Each team received access to a real Linux server and had to explore and configure the environment using various open-source technologies.

The challenge description emphasized experimentation, teamwork and technical problem solving in a Linux environment. The main technologies involved were:

- Linux

- Ansible

- Containers

- Podman

- Docker concepts

- Nginx

Participants were expected to work directly through the command line and use automation tools to manage and configure services on the server.

What we worked on

During the day, we explored the Linux server environment and worked with Podman containers and Ansible automation. The challenge required a combination of troubleshooting, experimentation and collaboration to make progress.

Although I already had some prior experience with Linux and containers, the hackathon gave me the opportunity to refresh existing knowledge while also learning new concepts and workflows throughout the day.

One aspect I particularly enjoyed was working on a real server instead of a purely theoretical setup. This made the challenge feel much more practical and realistic.

Reflection

I found this event very valuable because it combined multiple important aspects of IT in one experience:

- technical problem solving,

- teamwork,

- infrastructure management,

- and learning under time pressure.

Hackathons create a very different environment compared to traditional classes. Instead of following step-by-step exercises, we had to figure things out ourselves, search documentation, troubleshoot issues and make decisions as a team.

I especially appreciated the focus on Linux and automation because these technologies are widely used in modern infrastructure and DevOps environments. Working with tools such as Ansible and Podman also showed how important automation and containerization have become in current IT environments.

Another positive aspect was the interaction with companies and professionals present at the event. It was interesting to see how businesses organize technical challenges around technologies that are actively used in industry.

What I learned

This challenge helped me improve:

- Linux command line skills,

- troubleshooting abilities,

- understanding of containers,

- basic automation workflows with Ansible,

- and collaboration under time pressure.

It also reinforced the importance of documentation and experimentation when working with unfamiliar systems.

Would I participate again?

Definitely. Hack The Future was both educational and enjoyable. The combination of competition, teamwork and practical technology created a motivating atmosphere.

I would absolutely participate again in future hackathons because they are a great way to improve technical skills, gain practical experience and discover technologies outside of regular coursework.

item.title: Guest Lecture – Phishing Awareness and Ethical Phishing
item.description:
Guest Lecture – Phishing Awareness and Ethical Phishing

Recently, we attended a guest lecture about phishing presented by OutKept. The session focused on phishing awareness, social engineering and the importance of preparing organisations against one of the most common cyber threats today.

Content of the lecture

During the lecture, the speakers explained how phishing remains one of the most effective attack methods used by cybercriminals. Because phishing attacks mainly target human behaviour instead of technical vulnerabilities, awareness and training are extremely important.

OutKept demonstrated their phishing platform and explained how organisations can use phishing simulations and awareness campaigns to better educate employees and reduce the risk of successful attacks.

What I found most interesting was their concept of working with ethical phishers. Through the platform, ethical phishers can create phishing campaigns that are used for awareness and training purposes. If a phishing email used in a campaign receives clicks, the creator earns a bounty.

This creates an interesting incentive model:

- it rewards creativity,

- encourages realistic phishing simulations,

- and helps organisations stay up to date with modern phishing techniques.

The speakers explained that this approach helps them keep pace with malicious attackers, who are constantly improving their social engineering techniques.

Reflection

I found this lecture very relevant because phishing continues to be one of the biggest cybersecurity threats today. Even organisations with strong technical security can still become vulnerable when users are not sufficiently aware of phishing techniques.

The idea of ethical phishing was something I had not encountered before. I thought it was interesting to see how offensive security concepts can also be applied in a constructive and educational way.

The session also highlighted how important the human aspect of cybersecurity is. Technical protections alone are not enough; user awareness and behavioural training are equally critical.

This lecture further increased my interest in social engineering and security awareness topics. It was also interesting to see how companies are building innovative platforms around cybersecurity education and threat prevention.

Another aspect I appreciated was that the lecture was presented by two former Howest students from the same Applied Computer Science bachelor programme. It was motivating to see alumni working professionally in cybersecurity and contributing to innovative initiatives in the field.

Seeing former students build a platform focused on phishing awareness and ethical phishing also made the session feel more relatable. It showed how knowledge and interests developed during the programme can evolve into real-world cybersecurity projects and careers.

Future interest

At the end of the lecture, OutKept mentioned their upcoming Phishathon event. This sounded like a very interesting initiative because it combines creativity, ethical hacking and awareness training in a practical way.

I am definitely considering participating in the next edition, as it seems like a great opportunity to learn more about phishing techniques and improve practical cybersecurity skills.

Would I recommend it?

Yes. I would recommend this lecture to anyone interested in cybersecurity, especially students or professionals interested in phishing, social engineering or security awareness. The topic is highly relevant and the practical examples made the session engaging and easy to follow.

item.title: Tech & Meet – Cyber Threat Intelligence
item.description:
Tech & Meet – Cyber Threat Intelligence

On October 7th, I attended a free Tech & Meet evening session focused on Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI), presented by Niels Desloover and Sandro Manzo from the Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium (CCB).

The event was aimed at introducing participants to the concept of threat intelligence and showing practical examples of how organisations detect, investigate and respond to cyber threats today. Besides the presentations, the event also offered opportunities to network with people interested in cybersecurity and threat intelligence.

Content of the session

During the session, several important concepts related to Cyber Threat Intelligence were discussed. The speakers explained the different types of CTI and how threat intelligence can support active cyber protection.

One of the most interesting parts for me was the demonstration of practical investigations using Censys and pivoting techniques. The presenters showed how indicators such as hashes, fingerprints and CSS files can be used to discover related malicious infrastructure.

Some examples and techniques discussed during the session included:

  • Pivoting on fingerprint.js
  • Using SHA256 hashes and truncating them to 12 characters for searches
  • Searching for malicious infrastructure through shared CSS files such as alert.css
  • Investigating IP addresses using Censys queries
  • Filtering JA4S fingerprints on port 443 and HTTP traffic
  • The importance of avoiding overly strict ASN name filtering to prevent missing results

The speakers also discussed initiatives from the Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium such as:

  • Belgian Anti-Phishing Shield
  • Spear Warnings
  • Red Button

These examples demonstrated how CTI is applied in practice to detect phishing campaigns and malicious websites.

Reflection

I found this event very interesting because it directly connected to topics currently covered in my Cyber Threat Intelligence course this semester. Seeing practical demonstrations outside of the classroom helped me better understand how threat intelligence investigations are performed in real environments.

The technical demonstrations with Censys were especially useful because they showed how small indicators can be used to pivot towards larger malicious infrastructures. This made the investigative side of cybersecurity much more tangible.

I also appreciated that the session was accessible for both beginners and people with existing cybersecurity knowledge. Even though some concepts were technical, the explanations remained understandable.

This event confirmed my interest in threat intelligence and cybersecurity investigations. I would definitely attend similar sessions again because practical examples from professionals provide insights that are difficult to obtain through theory alone.
